title: Status Epilepticus

patient.name: Michael Torres
patient.persona: known epilepsy, medication non-adherent, brought by EMS after prolonged seizure

chief_complaint: EMS report: 34-year-old male with history of epilepsy. Generalized tonic-clonic seizure ongoing for 8 minutes prior to EMS arrival. EMS administered midazolam 10mg IM en route. Seizure stopped briefly but resumed. Total seizure time now approximately 25 minutes.

physical_exam.General: Unresponsive, ongoing generalized convulsions. Frothing at mouth. Incontinent of urine.
physical_exam.Airway: Sonorous respirations, jaw clenched, airway adjunct (OPA) in place by EMS. Suctioning required.
physical_exam.Lungs: Coarse breath sounds, rhonchi. SpO2 91% on non-rebreather 15L.
physical_exam.Neurologic: GCS 3 (E1V1M1) during seizure. Pupils 4mm, sluggish, equal. No focal signs during seizure. Hyperreflexia with clonus.
physical_exam.Extremities: Warm, diaphoretic. IV access established ×2 by EMS.

hidden_data.abg: pH 7.08, PaCO2 62, PaO2 58, HCO3 16, SaO2 89%. Severe mixed respiratory and metabolic acidosis.
hidden_data.cmp: Sodium 138, Potassium 4.8, BUN 18, Creatinine 1.1, Calcium 9.0, Magnesium 1.8, Glucose 130
hidden_data.ck: Creatine Kinase 850 U/L (elevated from muscle breakdown - rhabdomyolysis risk)
hidden_data.lactate: Lactate 6.5 mmol/L (elevated from prolonged muscle activity + hypoperfusion)
hidden_data.tox_screen: Negative for cocaine, amphetamines, PCP, opioids, benzodiazepines
hidden_data.ct_head: No acute hemorrhage. No mass. No midline shift. Old right temporal encephalomalacia (likely from prior trauma or prior seizure-related injury).
hidden_data.eeg: Continuous epileptiform discharges from right temporal lobe (focal onset with secondary generalization). Consistent with nonconvulsive status epilepticus after convulsions controlled.
hidden_data.cxr: Bilateral patchy infiltrates concerning for aspiration pneumonitis. No gross aspiration.

correct_diagnosis: Convulsive Status Epilepticus secondary to Medication Non-Adherence in Known Epilepsy, complicated by Respiratory Failure, Metabolic Acidosis, Aspiration Pneumonitis, and evolving Nonconvulsive Status Epilepticus
key_learning_points: Status epilepticus = continuous seizure >5 minutes OR recurrent seizures without return to baseline between episodes. It is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY with 10-20% mortality

Treatment follows a time-based protocol: 0-5 min: ABCs, check glucose, IV access. 5-20 min: Benzodiazepines (Lorazepam IV or Midazolam IM). 20-40 min: Second-line agents (Fosphenytoin, Valproate, Levetiracetam, Phenobarbital). >40 min: Refractory - ICU, continuous EEG, anesthetic agents (Propofol, Midazolam infusion, Pentobarbital)
After convulsions stop, 14-48% of patients have nonconvulsive status epilepticus on EEG (ongoing seizure activity without motor manifestations). Continuous EEG monitoring is essential
Complications: Respiratory failure (aspiration, apnea from benzodiazepines), metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, hyperthermia, cardiac arrhythmias, permanent neuronal injury
Common causes: Medication non-adherence in known epilepsy (most common), alcohol withdrawal, CNS infection, stroke, tumor, trauma, metabolic derangements

patient_responses.from_family: He's had epilepsy since a car accident in his 20s. He takes Keppra but he hasn't been taking it for about a week because he ran out and couldn't get a refill. He was fine this morning, then suddenly collapsed and started shaking.
patient_responses.seizure_history: He usually has one seizure every 3-4 months. They last 1-2 minutes and stop on their own. This has NEVER happened before where it doesn't stop.
patient_responses.medications: Levetiracetam (Keppra) 1500mg BID. No other medications. No allergies.
patient_responses.EMS_report: Seizure ongoing at arrival. Midazolam 10mg IM given. Seizure stopped for 2 minutes then resumed. Unable to obtain IV access initially, IO placed. Currently seizing for total 25 minutes.

hints.history: Seizure >5 minutes = status epilepticus. Ask about epilepsy history, anti-epileptic medications and adherence, last seizure, baseline seizure frequency, and potential triggers (missed doses, infection, sleep deprivation, alcohol).
hints.examination: ABCs FIRST. Protect airway (recovery position, suction, OPA/NPA). Check vitals including SpO2 and temperature. Rapid neurologic assessment between convulsions if possible.
hints.labs: Point-of-care glucose (rule out hypoglycemia). CBC, CMP, AED levels, toxicology screen, CK (rhabdomyolysis), lactate, ABG. CT head to rule out structural lesion. LP if febrile or first seizure.
hints.imaging: CT Head without contrast (urgently, to rule out hemorrhage, mass, stroke). MRI later when stabilized. Continuous EEG after convulsions controlled.

feedback.correct_message: Excellent management! You correctly recognized status epilepticus and followed the appropriate algorithm: ABCs, benzodiazepines (Lorazepam IV), and second-line AED loading (Fosphenytoin/Keppra). Recognizing the need for continuous EEG to detect nonconvulsive status and identifying medication non-adherence as the trigger shows strong clinical reasoning.
feedback.partially_correct_message: Good job starting benzodiazepines! However, this is status epilepticus (25 minutes of seizing) - benzodiazepines alone are insufficient after 5-10 minutes. You must proceed to second-line agents (Fosphenytoin, Valproate, or Levetiracetam IV load) and prepare for refractory management including ICU admission and continuous EEG.
feedback.incorrect_message: This is life-threatening status epilepticus. Lorazepam 4mg IV NOW (repeat once if still seizing in 5 minutes). If seizure persists after 2 doses of benzodiazepines: Load Fosphenytoin 20mg PE/kg IV (or Keppra 60mg/kg IV, or Valproate 40mg/kg IV). If still seizing after 40 minutes: ICU, intubate for airway protection, start continuous EEG, and induce burst suppression with Propofol or Midazolam infusion. Mortality is 10-20% without aggressive treatment.
clinical_pearls: Lorazepam IV is the gold standard benzodiazepine. If no IV access: Midazolam 10mg IM (auto-injector or nasal) - RAMPART trial showed IM midazolam is as effective as IV lorazepam
Fosphenytoin can cause hypotension and cardiac arrhythmias - infuse slowly (max 150mg PE/min) with cardiac monitoring
After convulsions stop, ALWAYS order continuous EEG. 14-48% of patients have ongoing nonconvulsive status epilepticus which causes permanent brain damage if untreated

glucose.comment: Normal glucose 128 rules out hypoglycemia as cause. Must check immediately in all seizure patients

ct_head.comment: No acute hemorrhage, no mass. Old right temporal encephalomalacia explains seizure focus

abg.comment: Severe mixed acidosis (pH 7.08, PaCO2 62, HCO3 16). Respiratory from hypoventilation + metabolic from lactate (seizure activity). Intubation indicated

antiepileptic_levels.comment: Undetectable Keppra levels confirm medication non-adherence as trigger

missing_tests: Continuous EEG monitoring after convulsions stop to detect nonconvulsive status epilepticus. MRI brain after stabilization to evaluate seizure focus
feedback.attending_note: Doctor, this is status epilepticus requiring immediate intervention: 1) Lorazepam 4mg IV push NOW (repeat in 5 min if still seizing). 2) Load Fosphenytoin 20mg PE/kg IV (or Levetiracetam 60mg/kg IV over 15 min). 3) If seizure persists after benzodiazepine + second agent: Intubate for airway protection and start Propofol infusion with continuous EEG monitoring. Target burst suppression on EEG. 4) Identify and treat trigger: Medication non-adherence - reload Keppra. 5) Monitor for complications: Serial CK for rhabdomyolysis, IV fluids to prevent ATN from myoglobinuria, treat hyperthermia with cooling blankets. 6) ICU admission for continuous EEG and ventilatory support. Neurology consultation.
